questions.question: What happens when you use a credit card to make a purchase?
options: Money is taken directly from your bank account
The bank pays the store and you owe the bank
You receive free money from the bank
The store gives you a loan
questions.explanation: When you use a credit card, the bank pays the store on your behalf. You then owe the bank that amount, which appears on your monthly statement.

questions.question: A credit card and a debit card work exactly the same way.
questions.explanation: A debit card takes money directly from your bank account, while a credit card borrows from the bank. They're used similarly at checkout, but the source of funds and repayment terms are very different.

questions.question: If you pay your full credit card balance by the due date, how much interest do you pay?
questions.explanation: If you pay your full balance by the due date, you get an interest-free grace period. You effectively borrowed the bank's money for free!

questions.question: What does APR stand for?
questions.explanation: APR stands for Annual Percentage Rate — it's the yearly interest rate charged on your unpaid credit card balance.

questions.question: Paying only the minimum payment on your credit card is a good long-term strategy.
questions.explanation: Paying only the minimum keeps you in debt for years and costs you much more in interest. It's designed to be the bare minimum to avoid penalties, not a healthy repayment plan.

questions.question: If you owe $3,000 at 22% APR and only pay the minimum ($60/month), approximately how long will it take to pay off?
questions.explanation: At minimum payments, it would take over 16 years to pay off $3,000 — and you'd pay about $4,800 in interest alone! That's why paying more than the minimum is crucial.

questions.question: What is the most important factor in your credit score?
options: How many credit cards you have
questions.explanation: Payment history accounts for 35% of your credit score — it's the single most important factor. Paying on time, every time, is the #1 way to build good credit.

questions.question: What percentage of your credit limit should you ideally use?
questions.explanation: Keeping your credit utilization under 30% (ideally 10-20%) shows lenders you're responsible with credit and helps build a higher credit score.

questions.question: Credit card rewards are always worth it, even if you carry a balance.
questions.explanation: If you carry a balance and pay interest, the interest charges almost always outweigh any rewards earned. Rewards are only truly valuable if you pay your full balance monthly.
